coredata.dc:title: Machine learning and genetic algorithm prediction of high quantum yield carbon quantum dots for chemical analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kannouma, Reham E.
Gab Allah, Ahmed M.
Kamal, Amira H.
Hammad, Mohamed A.
Mansour, Fotouh R.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are photoluminescent, small carbon nanomaterials with fast response time, excellent optical sensing properties, and have numerous applications in industrial and biomedical research. The main challenge in the preparation of CQDs is the production of nanomaterials with high quantum yield (QY), which guarantees high sensitivity and applicability. Yet, the widespread use of the trial-and-error approachin the production of CQDs is time and resource-consuming, besides producing large amounts of waste materials. Preparing CQDs in a controlled manner with predictable QY remains a significant challenge. This study examines the impact of various physico-chemical parameters and the atomic composition of precursors on the modelling of high-QY CQDs using machine learning models. The dataset is specifically created by fusing 117 data points from our own studies on QY and CQD synthesis. Following the training and assessment of several machine learning (ML) models, the Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) regression model was selected based on its lowest mean absolute error, root mean square error, and best coefficient of determination. The synthesis-related parameters were further optimized using the intelligent genetic algorithm to reach the highest possible QY of CQDs resulting in the preparation of CQDs with a QY of 79 %. Because of their outstanding optical characteristics, CQDs were used as ultra-sensitive luminescence sensors for Fe3+ ion detection. The probe had a detection limit of 302 nM and responded linearly to Fe3+ ions over a broad concentration range of 1.00–200.00 µM. The results of this study showed how well ML can direct the synthesis of superior CQDs, speeding up the creation of intelligent materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Machine learning
Genetic algorithm
Carbon quantum dots
Quantum yield
Ferric ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ML- model construction for QY prediction
Genetic algorithm optimization of the synthesis parameters

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Phase I: application of ML for the prediction of high QY-CQDs based on atomic composition and physicochemical properties; Phase II: the use of GA for optimization of synthesis-related parameters; Phase III: application of CQDs as a sensor for detection of Fe3+ ions.

(A) The heatmap of Pearson's correlation coefficient matrix among the input features of the microwave-grown CQDs, (B) t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) mapping of the training and the test dataset.
(A) HRTEM image (inset: Histogram of particle size distribution), (B) SAED pattern, (C) FTIR spectrum, and (D) EDX chart of the prepared CQDs.
Selectivity of CQDs toward different metal ions (n = 3).
Fluorescence spectra of CQDs in the presence of different Fe3+ concentrations (inset: calibration curve).
Calculated atomic composition and physicochemical properties of all used precursors using Marvin sketch [32].

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: PSA: polar surface area; MSA: molecular surface area; REF: refractivity; RBC: rotatable bond count; MPA: maximal projection area; MPR: maximal projection radius.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Accuracy, Intra- and inter-day precision data for the determination of Fe3+ by the developed method.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *Average of three replicates for each concentration.

head.ce:title: Machine learning and genetic algorithm prediction of high quantum yield carbon quantum dots for chemical analysis

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Elgeish Street, The Medical campus of Tanta University, Tanta 31111, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: The Medical campus of Tanta University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are photoluminescent, small carbon nanomaterials with fast response time, excellent optical sensing properties, and have numerous applications in industrial and biomedical research. The main challenge in the preparation of CQDs is the production of nanomaterials with high quantum yield (QY), which guarantees high sensitivity and applicability. Yet, the widespread use of the trial-and-error approachin the production of CQDs is time and resource-consuming, besides producing large amounts of waste materials. Preparing CQDs in a controlled manner with predictable QY remains a significant challenge. This study examines the impact of various physico-chemical parameters and the atomic composition of precursors on the modelling of high-QY CQDs using machine learning models. The dataset is specifically created by fusing 117 data points from our own studies on QY and CQD synthesis. Following the training and assessment of several machine learning (ML) models, the Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) regression model was selected based on its lowest mean absolute error, root mean square error, and best coefficient of determination. The synthesis-related parameters were further optimized using the intelligent genetic algorithm to reach the highest possible QY of CQDs resulting in the preparation of CQDs with a QY of 79 %. Because of their outstanding optical characteristics, CQDs were used as ultra-sensitive luminescence sensors for Fe3+ ion detection. The probe had a detection limit of 302 nM and responded linearly to Fe3+ ions over a broad concentration range of 1.00–200.00 µM. The results of this study showed how well ML can direct the synthesis of superior CQDs, speeding up the creation of intelligent materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All relevant datasets used in this study are available in the supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon nanomaterials that exhibit photoluminescence and have tiny sizes measuring less than 10 nm in diameter [1]. CQDs usually emit fluorescence when excited by ultraviolet light (UV) and their intensity can change when certain substances are present [2,3]. CQDs are more suitable than traditional fluorescent nano-sensors like heavy metal-containing quantum dots and organic fluorophores for quick and reversible sensing due to their fast response time [4,5]. In particular, CQDs possess several key advantages for optical sensing, including easy customization, wide-range light absorption, adjustable emission, strong durability, and minimal harm [6]. CQDs are fantastic nanostructures that have a variety of applications in both industrial and biomedical research [7–11]. In recent years, notable progress has been achieved in the development, characteristics, and real-world uses of CQDs. Nevertheless, a lot of researchers fail to recognize the importance of carbon sources, placing all their attention on the properties and applications of CQDs.

Various techniques can be used to create CQDs, including laser ablation, electrochemical carbonization, hydro/solvothermal treatment, and chemical oxidation. The drawbacks of these preparation techniques are the extreme conditions and lengthy process, while the microwave synthesis technique provides simplicity, quick reaction time, minimal energy usage, and consistent particle size [12–15,16]. To date, many chemicals, whether commercially available or derived from natural sources, have been used as precursors for the synthesis of CQDs [17,18]. These CQDs can exhibit various core structures, from crystalline and semicrystalline to amorphous, and can also be doped with elements such as oxygen, sulfur, nitrogen, and phosphorus [19,20]. They may display different surface groups, ligands, or fluorophores, as well as various size distributions, leading to distinct fluorescence mechanisms [21,22]. Research has demonstrated a viable method for detecting high-quality CQDs through their fluorescent quantum yield (QY) to highlight their superior sensing capabilities. The tuning of various parameters, such as synthesis-related or structure-related parameters, is crucial for the success of high-QYs CQDs. Designing or predicting the QY from the precursors’ atomic composition or physicochemical parameters is difficult due to the complexity of the reaction mechanism. Therefore, to produce high-QY CQDs, it is essential to understand the complex elemental structure and physicochemical properties of the precursors. A guided evolution approach is required to establish inherent correlations, utilizing transformative strategies. In this study, machine learning (ML) algorithms were utilized to tackle this obstacle.

Over the past ten years, ML has been used as a potent tool in various sectors of the field of chemistry. The ML algorithms can integrate numerous intricate and nonlinear connections to the target performance parameters [23]. It is better than traditional methods and tools because it allows for the extraction of hidden information in the raw data. It has the potential to delve deeper into discovering new materials, predicting properties, and optimizing synthesis [24]. Recently, there have been many ML models developed to forecast the fluorescence of CQDs and their application performance [25,26]. Using data from the literature, ML models like the extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) [27] and artificial neural networks (ANNs) [28] were used to predict the photoluminescence color. Training data may also be acquired by conducting synthesis experiments with various synthesis parameters, such as using p-benzoquinone (PBQ) and ethylenediamine (EDA) precursors [29], p-phenylenediamine and urea or p-phenylenediamine and citric acid [30], as well as p-dihydroxy benzene and EDA [31], were utilized to forecast color outcomes or enhance the QY of the CQDs through the utilization of XGBoost, ANNs, multilayer perceptron (MLP), support vector machine (SVM), and Gaussian process (GP) models.

Herein, for the first time, an ML-regression model was proposed to predict the QY of microwave-grown CQDs based on the atomic composition and physicochemical properties of ternary precursors. After predicting the CQDs with the highest QY, synthesis-related parameters were also optimized using a genetic algorithm (GA) to attain the highest possible QY from the recommended precursors. Moreover, as an illustration of the concept, the enhanced CQDs are employed as highly sensitive, quick, and precise sensors to detect ferric ions within a concentration range of 1.00–200.00 µM, with a detection limit of 302 nM (Fig. 1 ). In general, the ML-guided synthesis approach is crucial for innovative material design and swift synthesis in upcoming times. This study demonstrates the potential of combining ML models with experimental techniques to advance the preparation of highly luminescent CQDs for chemical analysis.

All the used reagents and their physicochemical constants are tabulated in Table 1 . Ferric chloride, zinc sulphate, cobalt nitrate, chromium chloride, ferrous sulphate, lead acetate, calcium chloride, magnesium chloride, sodium chloride, and potassium chloride were purchased from Piochem (The 6th of October City, Egypt). A mixture of 0.02 M acetic acid, 0.02 M phosphoric acid, and 0.02 M boric acid was used to prepare Britton-Robinson buffer, with the pH set at 3 using 1 M sodium hydroxide. Deionized water was used to prepare aqueous solutions.

Marvin sketch software was used for drawing, displaying and characterizing chemical structures of the used precursors, Marvin 17.21.0, Chemaxon [32]. A UV/Vis double-beam spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific model Evolution 201, USA) was used to perform spectrophotometric measurements. Fluorescence measurements were taken using a JASCO FP-8300 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) equipped with a 1-cm quartz cell and a 5 nm slit width, scanning at a speed of 1000 nm/min with low sensitivity. The Spectra Manager software (Jasco Co., Tokyo, Japan) was used for data processing and collection. A Thermo Fisher Scientific USA Thermo Scientific Talos F200i (S)TEM field emission (scanning) transmission electron microscope with a 200 kV acceleration voltage was utilized to study the shape and dimensions of CQDs. The researchers used a Burker Tensor 27 FT/IR spectrophotometer from Germany to analyze the surface components of CQDs. The CQDs were prepared using the TM-25MK Tornado Microwave oven (900 W). The weighing was carried out using the AXIS balance from Gdańsk, Poland (ALN220). A pH meter from Jenway (model 3510, Saint Neots, UK) was utilized for measuring pH levels. A combination of an Elmasonic-S 60H ultrasonic cleaning device and a Cyan-CL008 bench Centrifuge were utilized. The SUEZ water purification system from Paris, France was utilized for water deionization.

A mixture of ternary precursors was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water to give a final concentration of 0.1 M of each, sonicated for five minutes and heated in a microwave for 2 min at 900 W. The formed residue was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water before being sonicated for 5 min and centrifuged for 10 min at 6500 rpm. The supernatant of the centrifugate was filtered through a 0.22 µM syringe filter. Each synthesized CQD was scanned for the best excitation wavelength within the range of 280–380 nm and the QY was measured at the best one for each CQD. For sensing Fe3+ ions, the stock-filtered solution of the optimum CQD was diluted using deionized water to get a working solution of 2% v/v.

The QY of each CQD was determined immediately after its preparation by applying the following equation using the single-point approach [33]:
(1) Φ CQD = Φst × (FL CQD /FLst) × (η CQD /ηst) × (Ast/A CQD)

Where The QY of CQD, the integrated fluorescence intensity of CQD, the absorbance value of CQD, and the refractive index of the solvent are denoted by ΦCQD, FLCQD, ACQD, and η respectively, while Φst, FLst, Ast represent those of the standard. In 0.1 M H2SO4 solution, quinine sulphate (Φst= 0.54 at 360 nm) was used as the standard fluorescence material, with absorbance values being adjusted to 0.05.

Out of the total of 3C26=2600 combinations, 117 random combinations of three precursors were chosen for constructing the dataset. The dataset consisted of 117 trials prepared in the laboratory. In each trial, 17 input features of the three precursors are presented (%C, %H, %O, %N, %P, %S, PSA, MSA, REF, H doner, H acceptor, RBC, Fraction of sp3 C, polarizability, drieding energy, MPA, and MPR). The label of each trial was the measured QY value of the prepared CQD. Table S1 illustrates the descriptions of the input features for the used dataset.

For ML models, pre-processing is a crucial step that involves transforming raw data into a format suitable for modelling. Effective pre-processing can significantly improve the performance of machine learning algorithms. Here standardised features are applied by subtracting the mean and scaling them to have a unit variance. The standard score (z) of a feature x is calculated using the following formula:
(2)
z
=
x
-
μ
σ

where
μ
is the mean of the feature values, and
σ
is the standard deviation of the feature values. This calculation transforms the feature into a distribution with a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1, allowing for easier comparison across different features. Moreover, QY values were multiplied by ten to increase the performance of the prediction model.

As shown in Fig. S1, the MLP model with N0 inputs, a single hidden layer containing N1 neurons and an output layer with N2 neurons. Eq. (3) illustrates the calculation of the output layer (yj ):
(3)
y
j
=
φ
0
∑
i
= 1
N
0
w
i
,
j
x
j
+
b
j
j
= 1 , ⋯ ,
N
2

where
w
i , j
is the weight connecting the i hidden layer neuron to the output j output neuron,
b j
represents the bias for the output layer neuron, and
φ 0
is the activation function for the output layer. In this work, ReLU activation function is represented mathematically by
φ 0
=
m a x
( 0 , X )
, where the use of the ReLU function because it is a positive linear function, i.e., it produces output values in the range of [0,
X
]. A training phase is required for the MLP to learn and reliably predict the value of QY. In this work, we use two hidden layers with 64 and 2 number of neurons respectively and the layers activation function have a ReLU activation function. The most powerful optimizer, Adam optimizer [34], was selected for the training procedure. The learning rate (LR) was established at 0.001. The training model was configured to run for 1350 epochs.

Almost any system component can benefit from optimization. In this case, we can apply optimization to identify the optimal synthetic procedures for maximizing QY values from precursors (L-cysteine, citric acid, and diammonium hydrogen citrate) suggested by the MLP model. GA, an evolutionary algorithm widely used in computer science, offers effective solutions for optimization challenges. Five microwave-related parameters are selected as critical factors affecting the QY of CQDs: the concentrations of the three precursors, microwave time, and power. The dataset includes 35 different combinations of these parameters, each tested in the lab, with the measured QY values as labels. The objective function is set to maximize QY. The parameter boundaries and values are detailed in Table S2. A random forest regression model is employed within GA optimization to predict optimal process parameters, thereby maximizing the QY values.

16.2 mg of FeCl3 was dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water to obtain a stock solution of FeCl3 (1 mM). Different volumes of FeCl3 stock solution were added to 100 µL of CQD (2%) before the addition of 1 mL of Britton Robinson buffer (pH 3) and the volume was adjusted to 10 mL with deionized water to attain the final concentration of Fe3+ ions within the range of 1–200 µM. The fluorescence intensity at 420 nm was measured immediately after excitation at 350 nm. To construct the calibration curve, the F°/F values were plotted against corresponding Fe3+ ions concentrations (μM).

The dataset was created to predict the value of QY values for the training of ML algorithms and assess how accurately they predict the QY values. The calculation of feature correlations between pairs of features was demonstrated in Fig. 2 A, showing that low linear correlations confirmed the success of feature selection. The t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) method is a nonlinear dimensionality reduction approach specifically intended for embedding high-dimensional data into the space of lower-dimensional data [35,36]. Theinput features distributions of thegathered pairings, as determined by t-SNE, are displayed in Fig. 2B. Thefindings demonstratedthatthe distribution areas ofthe training and testing datasetswere similar,showing that the combinations with various features were structurally varied andthat the splitting of the sets wasappropriate.

Three commonly used metrics were used to evaluate the framework's effectiveness in regression tasks. The most crucial regression metrics are the mean absolute error (MAE) (Eq. (4)), the root mean square error (RMSE) (Eq. (5)), and the coefficient of determination (R2) (Eq. (6)). Their definition is as follows:
(4)
MAE =
∑
i = 1
n
P -
E
n
(5)
RMSE =
∑
i = 1
n
P - E
2
n
(6)
R
2
=
∑
i = 1
n
P - E
2
∑
i = 1
n
E
¯
- E
2

where P,
E
¯
, E and n are symbols for the predictive value, the mean of the experimental values, the specific experimental value, and the number of compounds, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ML- model construction for QY prediction
ce:section.ce:para: After the preparation of the dataset, various regression models including Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) regression, Random Forest (RF) regression, Adaptive Boosting (AdaBoost) regression, Bootstrap Aggregating (Bagging) regression, Support vector regression (SVR), and MLP were trained using the training set. To test the predictive performance of the regression models, we divided the original dataset randomly to 80% for training and 20% for testing. Next, metrics were computed to assess how well the models predicted outcomes. The effectiveness of these machine learning models was assessed based on MAE, RMSE, and R2.

The SGD is one of the most common machine learning algorithms for model optimization; it is a straightforward but highly effective method for training linear classifiers and regressors with convex loss functions, using a stochastic gradient algorithm. Although SGD has been present in the machine learning field for quite some time, it has only recently gained significant focus in the realm of large-scale learning [37].

The RF model, a machine learning ensemble model, has been applied in a variety of areas [38]. RFs are made up of tree predictors that rely on values from a random vector collected independently and with the same distribution across all trees in the forest. In this research, the “random forest” function was used to build the RF model [36]. The number of regression trees within the forest is 100.

AdaBoost is an ensemble technique that strengthens weak estimators to produce a stronger and more accurate regressor for the prediction of the process [39]. This method is commenced by fitting a regressor to the raw data values and fits more copies of the regressor to the same data, however, the weights of instances changed according to the error of the current prediction [40].

Bagging is an ensemble learning technique used to increase the stability and accuracy of machine learning models by merging several base learners. In the case of the Bagging Regressor, the base learner is usually a regression method. Bagging lowers overfitting and variation by training each base learner on a randomly chosen subset of the training data [41].

SVR, an extension of a support vector machine, is a generalized learning technique in pattern recognition, regression, and so on; as well as good performance for non-linear problems rooted in the idea of minimizing structural risk for pattern recognition purposes [42]. It is widely recognized as a supervised learning method that returns a continuous value output instead of discrete values [43], so it was chosen as a regression model for this work. It optimizes a tube around a continuous-valued function to minimize prediction error (the difference between predicted and true class labels) [44]. In an SVR, different types of kernels were used (radial basis function (RBF), linear, and Polynomial kernels) to map data into higher dimensions to fit the best hyperplane to the training data [45].

The MLP is a widely utilized neural network, renowned for its efficacy in both regression and classification. A feed-forward neural network with multiple layers architecture, including an input layer with N0 features, one or multiple hidden layers containing N1 nodes, and an output layer with only one node is appropriate for regression tasks. The MLP network may be trained to recognize patterns in complex nonlinear regressions by altering its synaptic weights using a learning technique like back propagation(BP)based on error correctionlearning [46].

After pre-processing the dataset, six ML models, SGD, RF, AdaBoost, Bagging, SVR and MLP algorithms were utilized based on the input features of the training set. The effectiveness of these ML models was assessed by the test set and presented in Fig. S2. The results indicated that the MLP algorithm showed the favourably RMSE and MAE of 20.03 and 742.07, moreover the highest R2 of 0.698. Most experimental values in Fig. S3 align with the line y = x, showing that the predicted values closely match the true values. As a result, the MLP model, which demonstrates exceptional predictive accuracy, is ultimately selected for additional analysis evaluation and implementation. Extracting the significance of each feature from the trained MLP model is crucial in fully understanding the relationship between atomic composition, physicochemical parameters of the precursors, and the QY of the prepared CQDs. The significance of a characteristic shows how greatly it adds to the forecast. The more significant a feature is, the more impact it has on the experimental result, specifically on the value of QY. As shown in Fig. S4, refractivity has the most significant role in the determination of the QY of the synthesized CQDs followed by polarizability. The MLP model was utilized to forecast the QY of 2483 potential combinations of three precursors, with seven combinations highlighted for their top predicted QY. Tests were conducted in the laboratory, and the findings are displayed in Table S3. A remarkable QY of 58.4% is reached, surpassing the highest QY of 25.5% in the training data set and ranking among the top reported QYs. These input features are related to the combination of L-cysteine, citric acid, and diammonium hydrogen citrate in an equimolar concentration of 0.1 M. This combination is further optimized in terms of synthesis-related parameters using GA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Genetic algorithm optimization of the synthesis parameters
ce:section.ce:para: One heuristic search and optimisation technique is the GA, which is based on natural evolution [47]. GA, widely applied in computational mathematics, provide outstanding solutions to optimization problems. Drawing inspiration from evolutionary biology principles like heredity, mutation, natural selection, and hybridization, GAs are ideal for addressing intricate optimization tasks [48]. It starts the operation with a set of random solutions, each solution represented by a string of symbols. Each solution is called a chromosome, represented by a binary number combination [49]. The fitting function is used to assess the suitability of a new chromosome. A new chromosome is generated using crossover through the merging of two parent chromosomes, mutation is used to adjust the chromosome. Finally, the best-fit chromosome is selected, whereas other chromosomes are discarded. Across succeeding generations, the GA strives to converge towards the fittest chromosome, representingthe best or near-best solution [50]. GA optimisation is used to discover the optimal synthesis-related parameters that maximise the QY across diverse laboratory conditions. Five microwave-related parameters are selected as significant parameters for the production of a high QY-CQD as presented in Table S4. It is well known that the concentration of the precursors can affect the QY of the synthesized CQD besides the microwave time and power. 35 different combinations of the different levels of the parameters were carried out in the lab and used as a dataset for GA. Different predictive models (RF,MLP, SVR”linear and poly’’) within GA optimization were tried and evaluated in terms of R2, andRMSE. RF was selected as the predictive model due to its highest R2 value (0.9764) and lowest RMSE (4.53). The optimal synthetic conditions were figured out as shown in Table S5. These conditions were further tried in the lab producing CQD with QY of 79 % (vs. 83.19 % predicted by the GA).

HRTEM images depicted evenly distributed spherical particles of the produced CQDs without any visible clumping. The particle size distribution histogram indicated sizes between 6 and 16 nm, with an average size of 12 nm (Fig. 3 A). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image showed that the CQDs were in an amorphous state as shown in Fig. 3B. The surface groups were analyzed using FT-IR (Fig. 3C), and the peaks found were N-H at 3426 cm−1, O-H at 3426 cm−1 (broad), and S-H at 2100 cm−1. These functional groups have the potential to enhance the water compatibility and durability of the CQDs in a water-based environment [51]. The presence of carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing functional groups was confirmed by the bands at 1641 cm−1 for C=O and 1152 cm−1 for C–O [52]. Elemental analysis with Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) revealed that CQDs contained 20.8% carbon, 57.1% oxygen, 12.2% nitrogen, and 9.8% sulfur, showing successful doping (Fig. 3D). The emission of synthesized CQDs showed variation in response to excitation in the 280–380 nm range (Fig. S5A). The optical characteristics of the CQDs were studied through the UV spectrum (Fig. S5B), revealing a single peak at 250 nm associated with π-π * transitions and another peak at 348 nm related to n-π* transitions [53,54]. Following excitation at 350 nm, the highest level of fluorescence was observed at 420 nm, with a QY of 79% (Fig. S5B).

Lately, there has been a swift advancement in the development of ion detection utilizing CQDs fluorescence [55,56]. Out of the 10 ions chosen, only the Fe3+ ion demonstrated a noticeable decrease in fluorescence for CQDs as shown in Fig. 4 , displaying a strong response to Fe3+ ion sensitivity. The potential quenching mechanism involves Fe3+ ions having a high attraction to N, and O functional groups in CQDs, as well as engaging in electron transfer with the optically active sites of CQDs [57]. The quenching conditions were optimized in terms of the volume of CQDs, pH, and incubation time. For optimization of the volume of CQDs, different volumes were tried (50, 75, 100, 125, and 150µL). The highest quenching effect was achieved with 100 µL (Fig. S6A), which is thus used in subsequent optimization processes. Additionally, pH is an important factor influencing CQD fluorescence quenching, making its optimization essential [58]. Different pH values were studied in the range of 2 to 9 using Britton Robinson buffer. As presented in Fig. S6B, pH 3 was the optimum condition for the quenching of CQD fluorescence in the presence of Fe3+ ions. Finally, the incubation time was studied by measuring the fluorescence intensity every one minute for 20 min. Fig. S6C reveals that the interaction between the CQDs and Fe3+ ions occurs immediately and remains stable for 20 min. These optimum conditions were used for the subsequent validation of the method. The method validation process followed the guidelines outlined in ICH Q2 R2 [59], which included evaluations of linearity and range, the limit of detection (LOD), the limit of quantitation (LOQ), accuracy, and precision. Moreover, the method showed a strong linear correlation between the F°/F ratio and Fe3+ (1-200µM) concentrations, with R2 of 0.9976 (Fig. 5 ). LOD and LOQ were assessed through mathematical calculations, where LOD equals 3.3x standard deviation of the intercept/slope of the calibration curve while LOQ equals 10x standard deviation of the intercept/slope of the calibration curve. LOD represents the lowest analyte concentration that can be accurately identified. The LOQ is the minimum level that can be measured accurately and precisely. LOD and LOQ were calculated at 302 and 917 nM, respectively. The developed method's accuracy was assessed by conducting three determinations at three different concentrations (5, 80, 160 µM) within the linear range. Table 2 showed that the average percentage of recoveries fell within the acceptable compendial range, confirming the accuracy of the method. Intra and inter-day precision were evaluated with three replicates of three different concentrations on the same day and three consecutive days, respectively. The results obtained demonstrated an acceptable %RSD of under 2% as indicated in Table 2. In the end, the CQDs that are ready offer a sensitive and easy-to-use method for detecting Fe3+ ions.

To cover the gap related to the prediction of the QY of the synthesized CQDs based on the atomic composition and the physicochemical properties of the precursors, the current study benefits from the MLP ML model for successfully guiding the controlled fabrication of high-QY CQDs eliminating the drawbacks of the trial-and-error method. A total of 117 synthesis data points were taken from our laboratory experiments to create the dataset. The best-performing MLP regression model is then developed, and it has a 20% error rate when predicting the QY of the CQDs. The critical elements in the QY prediction of CQDs can be deducedwith the use of feature importance from the MLP model. The most important factor in determining the QY of the produced CQDs is refractivity followed by polarizability. Following the MLP model prediction of the optimum ternary combination of the precursors (L-cysteine, citric acid and diammonium hydrogen citrate), the synthesis-related parameters, including the concentration of each precursor, microwave power and microwave time are intelligently optimized by employing GA. The error between the predicted QY (83.1%) and the measured QY (79%) is only less than 5%. Therefore, controlled synthesis for high-QY CQDs is accomplished. The fabricated CQDs were furthermore characterized using several techniques to study the surface structure and the elemental composition of CQDs through HRTEM, FTIR, and EDX. Several metals were screened to be detected using the fabricated probe. The highest quenching was attained in the presence of Fe3+ ions. The quenching process was optimized in terms of the volume of CQDs, pH and the incubation time. The probes showed a wide linearity range (1.00–200.00 µM, R2=0.9976) with a detection limit of 302 nM, demonstrating its highly selective and sensitive performances. Fortunately,the method described in this paper is considered an initial stage towards developing artificial intelligence methods for analysing and improving the material preparation process. The idea of the current study is the application of a dependable artificial general intelligence system for the field of nanomaterials research which can be simply extended to numerous other potential systems in the future.

Reham E. Kannouma: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ahmed M. Gab Allah: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Software programming, Data curation, Conceptualization, Visualization. Amira H. Kamal: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Mohamed A. Hammad: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fotouh R. Mansour: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the assistance with the characterization provided by the Main Chemical Lab (MCL), Cairo, Egypt.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112499.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

Kolekar, Quick and low cost synthesis of sulphur doped carbon dots by simple acidic carbonization of sucrose for the detection of Fe3+ ions in highly acidic environment, Diam. Relat. Mater. 88 (2018) 262–268. doi: 10.1016/J.DIAMOND.2018.07.018.

Eng. 6 (2018) 12668–12674. doi: 10.1021/acssuschemeng.8b01625.
